summary: DHH reflects on his year of challenging multiple tech industry orthodoxies simultaneously — cloud computing economics, SaaS dominance, and JavaScript build tooling. He argues that shipping working alternatives is more threatening to paradigm defenders than mere intellectual debate, because concrete proof is harder to dismiss than abstract arguments. He identifies the motte-and-bailey fallacy as a common tactic used by defenders of the status quo, such as shifting from 'the cloud is cheaper' to 'it's about agility' when challenged. He concludes by acknowledging everyone is susceptible to this kind of defensive thinking and advocates for reframing being wrong as learning.
key_points: Shipping working counterexamples is more powerful than intellectual arguments because concrete proof can't be dismissed with rhetoric
Paradigm defenders employ the motte-and-bailey fallacy, advancing bold claims then retreating to weaker but defensible positions when challenged
People who stake their identity on a particular view of reality perceive working alternatives as existential threats
The antidote to paradigm-guarding is reframing 'being wrong' as 'learning more' to overcome natural defensive instincts
money_quotes: If you've staked your identity on a certain view of reality, say, The Cloud Is The Future, or SaaS Will Reign Forever, or Bundling JavaScript Is A Must, it's a threat when someone dares not just argue, but ship an irrefutable counterpoint.
Shipping proof isn't so easily refuted by clever rhetoric or calls to authority.

Even if the shipping thesis is limited in scope, it still has the quality of a black swan against the postulate that "all swans are white". It takes just one flapping bird of the wrong color to undermine a categorical premise.
That's why you see such furious activity around policing the borders of the paradigm. Why the goal posts are constantly moving to ensure no striker can ever be said to have scored.

The bailey is "the cloud is always cheaper" and the motte is "actually it's about agility". You chase a compelling argument because you know it's persuasive, but then retreat to the less compelling argument when it comes under attack.
Changing our minds about things we believe is hard! Even if the evidence appears to warrant such a change.
I try to combat that natural human instinct by forcing myself to see "being wrong" as "learning more".
key_insight: Shipping a working counterexample is the most powerful way to challenge an entrenched paradigm, because concrete proof cannot be deflected by the rhetorical retreats that paradigm guardians rely on.
